author: @RichardCranium321
text: have you heard about the industrial carbon dioxide mining plan to extract carbon from the air then turn it into graphene? It will use a lot of energy, but should help reduce emissions as well.  right now, the problem is diminishing returns, but if we could get back to (and sustain) 2019 energy costs then we might see some real progress.

replies.author: @greenghost6416
replies.text: lul

replies.author: @AutoNomades
replies.text: How do you reduce pollution by consuming more energy ? ^^

replies.author: @RichardCranium321
replies.text:
@AutoNomades if the energy you're using to run the carbon extraction is based on something like hydroelectric, or if they're located in places like Germany where they often have an excess of energy due to solar and wind production from late spring to early fall... they're forced to sell that energy or waste it and then they have to buy it back later in the winter months  -  But that excess energy could be used to extract carbon and produce graphene for industrial use or even to create graphene battery banks for storing that excess energy in the future.  

Graphene mining for batteries in Germany would be far more ethical and less harmful to the environment than Lithium (or cobalt) mining in Africa for batteries from China

replies.author: @RichardCranium321
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I knew it was an arc welder but I couldn't tell what the rod was.  Have you seen the NightHawkInLight video where he made graphene? Thought I remembered him using graphite rods from a battery.

I wish I had science teachers like you and him when I was in school. My chemistry and physics teacher was the same person:  an uglier, meaner version of Ellen Degenerate...  the coolest thing she ever did was the thermal exchange demonstrations where you crush a soda can using a hot plate and ice water. Aside from that it was a total snooze fest and people often fell asleep
